title: Annual Health Review Made Simple
summary: Prepare for checkups with a one-page list of symptoms, goals, and medication updates.
body: Start one week before your annual visit by writing your top three concerns in plain language. Opening with your priorities helps the appointment stay focused and useful.
Bring all medications and supplements, including over-the-counter products and recent dose changes. A complete list prevents duplicate prescriptions and harmful interactions.
Track symptoms that changed in the last three months, including sleep, appetite, pain, memory, balance, and mood. Dates matter because they help your clinician spot patterns.
Prepare one page with specialist visits, emergency visits, and test results since your last annual review. This saves time and reduces the chance that important context is missed.
Ask for a preventive care check: vaccines, bone health, blood pressure targets, vision, hearing, and cancer screening timelines. Confirm what is due now versus later this year.
If cost is a concern, ask directly whether lower-cost medication alternatives are appropriate. Price barriers are common and discussing them early improves adherence.
Before leaving, repeat back your plan: medication changes, next tests, and who you should contact for questions. This quick teach-back reduces confusion once you get home.
End with one question: what changes should trigger a call before my next appointment? Put those warning signs in writing and keep them on your refrigerator.

seo.title: Retiree Annual Health Visit Checklist
seo.description: A simple and respectful checklist for annual health appointments.
